coredata.dc:title: One-step self-assembled epoxide-containing nanodots as an enzyme-immobilized platform for biosensing
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Pei-Ying
Hsieh, Shuchen
coredata.dc:description: 3-Glycidoxypropyl trimethoxysilane-containing self-assembled SiOx nanodots (GPS-SANDs) bearing epoxide functional groups were successfully fabricated via a facile one-step heat treatment process. The GPS-SANDs possessed spherical-like particles with an average diameter of 3.1±0.3nm. This material exhibited a broad band, and a multicolor. Glucose oxidase (GOx) enzyme was further immobilized on GPS-SANDs via aminolysis of the epoxide groups to detect glucose using photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. The PL intensity changed inversely with the glucose concentration. The glucose detection limit of GOx-immobilized GPS-SANDs was determined to be 100μM. Therefore, GPS-SANDs provide a promising GOx-immobilized platform for the design of optical glucose biosensors with high sensitivity. In addition, this platform may allow for the further development of enzyme biosensing systems through conjugations between GPS-SANDs and a target enzyme.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Epoxy
Nanodots
Glucose
Sensor
Photoluminescence

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characteristics of the GPS-SANDs particles. (a) AFM topography image of GPS-SANDs deposited on a silicon wafer substrate (1×1μm2 scan). (b) Particle size distribution histogram of GPS-SANDs particles. (c) TEM image of GPS-SANDs particles. (d) PL spectra of a GPS-SANDs solution recorded over progressively longer excitation wavelengths (λex) from 340 to 540nm in 20nm increments.

PL spectra of an alkylamine-functionalized GPS-SANDs solution recorded for excitation wavelengths ranging from 380 to 540nm in 20nm increments: (a) C3N-GPS-SANDs, (b) C12N-GPS-SANDs and (c) C18N-GPS-SANDs.

Normalized PL spectra of GPS-SANDs (black line). The blue line shows the PL spectra of free GOx and the red line shows the PL spectra of the GOx-conjugated GPS-SANDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
SERS spectra of GPS-SANDs, GOx and GOx-GPS-SANDs.
(a) PL spectra of GOx-GPS-SANDs in the presence of H2O2. (b) Linear response of the PL intensity ratio (I0 −I)/I0 at different H2O2 concentrations (2, 4, 6, 8 and 10mM).

(a) PL spectra of GOx-GPS-SANDs, (b) the plots of the relative fluorescence intensity of immobilized GOx-GPS-SANDs versus the concentration of glucose (8–800μM). The inset displays the linear relationship between (I0 −I)/I0 and the concentration of glucose between 88μM–400μM. (b) PL spectra of GPS-SANDs with free GOx, (b) the plots of the relative fluorescence intensity of GPS-SANDs with free GOx versus the concentration of glucose (8–800μM). The inset displays the linear relationship between (I0 −I)/I0 and the concentration of glucose between 88μM–400μM.

head.ce:title: One-step self-assembled epoxide-containing nanodots as an enzyme-immobilized platform for biosensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Center for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, National Sun Yat-Sen University, 70 Lien-Hai Road, Kaohsiung 80424, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Center for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, College of Pharmacy, Kaohsiung Medical University, 100 Shih-Chuan First Road, Kaohsiung 80708, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Stem Cell Research, Kaohsiung Medical University, 100 Shih-Chuan First Road, Kaohsiung 80708, Taiwan

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry and Center for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, National Sun Yat-Sen University, Kaohsiung, 80424, Taiwan.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: 3-Glycidoxypropyl trimethoxysilane-containing self-assembled SiOx nanodots (GPS-SANDs) bearing epoxide functional groups were successfully fabricated via a facile one-step heat treatment process. The GPS-SANDs possessed spherical-like particles with an average diameter of 3.1±0.3nm. This material exhibited a broad band, and a multicolor. Glucose oxidase (GOx) enzyme was further immobilized on GPS-SANDs via aminolysis of the epoxide groups to detect glucose using photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. The PL intensity changed inversely with the glucose concentration. The glucose detection limit of GOx-immobilized GPS-SANDs was determined to be 100μM. Therefore, GPS-SANDs provide a promising GOx-immobilized platform for the design of optical glucose biosensors with high sensitivity. In addition, this platform may allow for the further development of enzyme biosensing systems through conjugations between GPS-SANDs and a target enzyme.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent nanoparticles, such as semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), exhibit a strong photoluminescence (high quantum yields), multiple fluorescence and excellent photostability [1–3]. Based on their excellent optical properties, QDs have attracted much attention in various areas, such as bioimaging, catalysis, biosensing [4,5], and especially glucose sensing [6,7]. Various types of quantum dots (e.g., CdSe/ZnS QDs, CdTe QDs, Silicon QDs and Carbon QDs) have been used to monitor glucose based on their fluorescence quenching by H2O2 in assembled QDs-glucose oxidase (GOx) complexes [6,8–12]. Nevertheless, some QDs require modification by a secondary chemical process because they are toxic, environmentally unsafe and chemically unstable as well as being susceptible to ultraviolet light excitation [11,13,14]. Importantly, the development of novel fluorescence-enhanced nanomaterials with excellent selectivity and sensitivity to serve as efficient fluorescent probes is desirable.

Recently, several groups have focused on the design of QD-based on optical sensing systems for glucose determination. CdSe/ZnS QDs have been used for the specific and quantitative detection of glucose based on the quenching the photoluminescence (PL) emission of the QDs by H2O2 and acidic changes by GOx-catalyzed oxidation of glucose [10]. Carbon QDs have been prepared from polyethylene glycol for use as a PL probe for glucose detection based on PL enhancement with H2O2 produced from GOx-catalyzed oxidation of glucose [11]. Ionic liquids may catalyze H2O2 decomposition into radicals, which could quench the fluorescence of CdSe QDs [8]. A medium pH and heavy metal ions (i.e., Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+) may affect the GOx activity and PL intensity [15]. The assembly consisting of Concanavalin (ConA)-conjugated CdTe QDs and thiolated β-cyclodextrin-modified Au nanoparticles was constructed for use as a nanobiosensor for glucose detection from human serum with good selectivity [16]. CdTe QDs-Au NPs on mesoporous silica microspheres have been fabricated for glucose detection. Au NPs in a mesoporous silica shell were able to catalytically oxidize glucose as a mimic of glucose oxidase (GOx). The resulting hydrogen peroxide can quench the photoluminescence (PL) signal of the QDs in a microsphere core [17].

A promising strategy for biosensor design is the immobilization of an enzyme onto a platform or mediator [18]. Nanostructures can play an important role in the immobilization of biomolecules due to their high effective surface area, enhancement of mass transport, suitable microenvironment and good conductivity [19]. Enzymes can be immobilized through crosslinking, adsorption, covalent binding, crystallization, entrapment and encapsulation [20]. GOx is a homodimer flavoprotein containing two active sites per molecule. GOx can be chemically functionalized with other functional groups, such as covalent bonding with carboxylic acid groups using nucleophiles (e.g., amine or hydroxyl groups), covalent attachment of the epoxide groups via ring opening reactions with amines (aminolysis) and non-covalent functionalization via van der Waals or π–π interactions [21]. However, the enzymatic glucose sensors in which GOx is immobilized on various materials suffer from insufficient stability and loss of enzyme activity during the immobilization process [22].

In our previous study, one-step self-assembled SiOx nanodots (SANDs) were prepared via hydrolysis and a condensation reaction using alkoxysilane [23] and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane [24] as molecular precursors. These nanodots exhibited band adsorption, multicolor fluorescence and very low toxicity. This combination of properties was exploited in an intravital imaging experiment using zebrafish [23] and cell cultures [24]. In this study, 3-glycidoxypropyl trimethoxysilane (GPS), which contains reactive epoxide groups that are widely used to conjugate with thiol-, amine- or hydroxyl-containing ligands in bioconjugate applications, was used as precursor to prepare GPS-containing SANDs (GPS-SANDs) through a one-step heat treatment process for amine conjugation and enzyme immobilization. GPS-SANDs exhibited multicolor photoluminescence and was further applied for the immobilization of GOx via aminolysis under ambient conditions to prevent disruption of the enzyme activity. The fluorescence of GOx-immobilized GPS-SANDs (GOx-GPS-SANDs) was quenched by H2O2, which was produced from the GOx-catalyzed oxidation of glucose. The fabricated GPS-SANDs demonstrated the possibility of preparing a novel platform for an enzyme biosensor system, and further application using bioconjugation is expected to produce a platform for use as an optical biosensor.

GPS-SANDs were synthesized using a one-step heat treatment process. GPS (3mL, Sigma-Aldrich) was placed into a thoroughly clean glass vial and heated at a constant temperature of 350°C with stirring at 1000rpm for 90min under an ambient air atmosphere. Then, the sample was cooled to room temperature with continuous stirring to afford GPS-SANDs.

The PL spectra were acquired using a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer. The excitation wavelengths were 340–540nm at 20nm intervals in the excitation domain. The emission spectra were recorded from 200 to 800nm. Atomic force microscopy (AFM, MFP-3D, Asylum Research) was performed to analyze the particle size of GPS-SANDs. A GPS-SANDs/toluene solution (10μL, Ratio of GPS-SANDs to toluene was 1:200V/V) was dropped and casted on a silicon wafer substrate. An atomic force microscope was operated in tapping mode under ambient conditions. A silicon cantilever (Olympus, AC240TS) with a nominal spring constant of 2N/m was used to obtain all of the images at a scan rate of 1.0Hz and an image resolution of 512×512 pixels. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of GPS-SANDs were acquired using a JEOL JEM-2100 operated at 200kV under a vacuum of 2×10−5 Pa. The TEM sample was prepared by depositing a GPS-SANDs solution (0.5μL) onto a TEM carbon grid with 200mesh copper (No. 01801, Ted Pella Inc.) and allowed to dry. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, JEOL JPS 9010 MX) was performed with a monochromatic Mg Kα X-ray radiation source to determine the elemental compositions of the GPS-SANDs sample, and the sample was prepared by placing a GPS-SANDs (5μL) solution onto clean gold substrates at room temperature. Surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) was performed to analyze the functional groups on the samples. The samples were deposited on a gold substrate and then dried under ambient temperature. The SERS experiment was performed using a Raman microscope (WiTec alpha 300R) with a 532nm incident laser and 40mW of power. A holographic grating (600g/mm) and a 1024×127 pixel back-illuminated CCD detector with a 5s total accumulation time were used.

GPS-SANDs (300μL) were mixed with alkylamine (6μg) which included propylamine (C3N, TCI America) dodecylamine (C12N, TCI America) and octadecylamine (C18N, TCI America). Then, all of the mixtures were sonicated at room temperature for 30min. The PL of the obtained samples was investigated.

GOx (2mg, Sigma) was dissolved in a phosphate buffer solution (10μL, Sigma). GPS-SANDs (590μL) was added to the GOx solution and further sonicated for 30min to afford GOx-GPS-SANDs. The GOx-GPS-SANDs was further analyzed using PL and SERS to confirm the crosslinking between GOx and GPS-SANDs.

The GOx-GPS-SANDs was mixed with 1μL of glucose at various different concentrations ranging from 8 to 800μM. The mixture was sonicated for 20min at room temperature. Then, the oxidation reaction of glucose with GOx-GPS-SANDs was investigated by PL at an excitation wavelength of 400nm. For comparison, free GOx was mixed with 1μL of glucose at various concentrations ranging from 8 to 800μM and the mixture was allowed to react for 20min. Then free GOx-glucose solution was used to directly mix GPS-SANDs, and then characterized by PL at an excitation wavelength of 400nm.

The particle size distribution of the GPS-SANDs particles was investigated using AFM topography image (Fig. 1 a). Fig. 1b shows the corresponding particle size histograms. The GPS-SANDs particles were well dispersed on the silicon surface, indicating minimal aggregation in solution, and exhibited spherical-like particles with a narrow size distribution and an average diameter of 3.1±0.3nm based on statistical analysis of 250 particles. This particle size distribution was in agreement with the particle size (i.e., 3.1±1.4nm) obtained from a high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1c). Because nanoparticles can exhibit bright and multicolor photoluminescence [25], they are capable of being applied as an important tool for biomedical applications, e.g., biolabeling, biosensing and bioimaging [26,27]. PL spectroscopy was used to characterize the optical properties of GPS-SANDs. Fig. 1d shows the PL spectra of a GPS-SANDs solution that was investigated under excitation wavelengths (λex) that varied from 340 to 540nm with a 20nm increment. The GPS-SANDs solution exhibited a broad band and multicolor fluorescence emission ranging from 480 (blue) to 580nm (yellow). Moreover, the intensity of the band depends on the excitation wavelength. The PL peak exhibited a maximum fluorescence emission (λem) of 505nm at λex of 420nm.

The result from the XPS spectra of GPS-SANDs are shown in Fig. S1. GPS-SANDs primarily contained Si 2p, O 1s and C 1s. The Si 2p peak that was located at 104.8eV was derived from silicon and SiO2 [28]. The O 1s spectrums that was located at 532.4 and 534.8eV indicated the presence of SiO and COC bonds, respectively. The C 1s spectrum that was located at 287 and 288.8eV was attributed to COC (epoxy group) and COSi bonding environments, respectively [29]. As a result, the GPS-SANDs were composed of SiO2 clusters with ether and epoxide functional groups.

Based on these results, the proposed synthesis of GPS-SANDs is shown in Fig. S2. The methoxy groups (CH3 −O) in the GPS precursor were hydrolyzed via a one-step heat treatment. Then, the hydrolyzed GPS molecules self-polymerized via a condensation reaction to form GPS-SANDs particles containing SiO2 clusters with ether and epoxide functional groups.

Epoxide functional groups can spontaneously react with the amino group of an amine (e.g., alkanolamine and alkylamine) through aminolysis in the absence of any catalyst in high yields [30,31]. In this study, GPS-SANDs was covalently functionalized with a series of alkylamine (propylamine, C3N; dodecylamine, C12N; octadecylamine, C18N), and PL spectroscopy was used to acquire the alkylamine-functionalized GPS-SANDs. Fig. 2 shows the PL spectra of the alkylamine-functionalized GPS-SANDs using an λex that varied from 380 to 540nm with 20nm increment. The emission spectra of alkylamine-functionalized GPS-SANDs was different from those of the original GPS-SANDs (Fig. 1d). In addition, the different chain lengths of the alkylamine resulted in different PL spectra. The emission range and λem of the alkylamine-functionalized GPS-SANDs shifted to a longer wavelength as the alkylamine chain length increased, corresponding to an increase in the particle size (Table S1). However, when the number of carbons in the alkylamine chain more than 12, only the intensities of the PL spectra changed, and the emission range and λem remained constant (Fig. 2). In a previous study, the shifts and intensity increase in the PL spectra changed with the chain length and particle size [23,32–34]. Therefore, the changes in the PL spectra as a function of the chain length and particle size were employed to detect the conjugation of the amino-containing biomolecules (e.g., enzymes) on GPS-SANDs.

Enzyme immobilization, which involves anchoring enzyme molecules in a platform or substrate, offers a potential solution to reduce the cost through enzyme stabilization [35,36]. For applications of GPS-SANDs in enzyme immobilization, GOx was immobilized on GPS-SANDs via an aminolysis reaction between the amino groups on GOx and the epoxide groups on GPS-SANDs. Fig. 3 shows PL spectra of GPS-SANDs and GOx-GPS-SANDs. No PL spectrum was observed for GOx. The PL spectrum of GOx-GPS-SANDs shifted to a longer wavelength (497nm) due to enlargement of the GPS-SANDs particle size with immobilized GOx.

The functional groups of GPS-SANDs, GOx and GOx-GPS-SANDs were determined using SERS. Fig. 4 shows the results from the Raman spectra of GPS-SANDs, GOx and GOx-GPS-SANDs. For GPS-SANDs, the strong Raman bands observed at 1268, 1475 and 1489cm−1 are characteristic of epoxide ring vibrations [37]. For GOx, the broad peaks at 1258, 1672 and 1470cm−1 were assigned to amide III, amide I and CH2/CH3 deformation, respectively [38]. The reductions in the peak intensity at 1268, 1475 and 1489cm−1 after immobilizing GOx on GPS-SANDs resulted from crosslinking between the amino groups on the GOx molecule and the epoxide groups on GPS-SANDs via aminolysis. We also evaluated XPS to confirm the conjugation of GPS-SANDs and GOx. As demonstrated in Supplementary Fig. S3, the N 1s of pure GOx has the main component at about 400eV, which is attributed to the primary amine group (NH). The peak position at 402.0-402.8eV was due to protonated amine groups (−NH3 +), which are attributed to amino acids [39]. When the GOx conjugated GPS-SAND, a new peak appeared at 397.3eV and is assigned to the secondary amine group (R2-NH) or tertiary amine group (R3-N) [40]. This is attributed to the GPS-SANDs binding glucose oxidase via the ring-opening reaction of oxirane groups with the amine moieties of the enzyme. Based on the results from the PL, XPS and Raman spectra, GOx was successfully immobilized on GPS-SANDs.

To test for glucose with high sensitivity and specificity, the use of GOx is preferred [7]. In general, the enzymatic oxidation of glucose produces gluconic acid and H2O2 with GOx catalyzing the oxidation reaction [22]. The GOx can be used for sensitive glucose evaluation with PL based on effective quenching by H2O2 [6,15]. The change in the PL intensity of GOx-GPS-SANDs with varying H2O2 concentrations is shown in Fig. 5 . The PL intensity gradually decreased as the H2O2 concentration increased. Moreover, the PL quenching efficiency increased as the H2O2 concentration (Fig. 5a). The PL quenching efficiency was linearly correlated with the H2O2 concentration, which varied from 2mM to 10mM (Fig. 5b). Therefore, the GOx-GPS-SANDs possessed the ability to detect H2O2 as well as the product of glucose oxidation catalyzed by GOx. Next, a sensing system using GOx-GPS-SANDs was further assessed for the detection of glucose.

The ability of the GOx-GPS-SANDs sensing system to detect glucose was evaluated at λex of 400nm in a glucose concentration range of 8–800μM. Fig. 6 shows the change of the PL intensity of GOx-GPS-SANDs after glucose treatment. The PL intensity gradually decreased as the glucose concentration increased (Fig. 6a). The energies of the valence and conduction band edges may have changed due to quantum confinement, which facilitated electron transfer from GOx-GPS-SANDs to H2O2 [13,22]. Positive [11,15,16] and negative [12,22] changes in the PL intensity of the quantum dots- or nanodots-GOx systems for glucose detection have been previously reported. The change in the PL peak at λem =497nm as a function of the glucose concentration is shown in Fig. 6b. The linear relationship between (I0-I)/I0 and glucose concentration was observed in the range of 88–400μM, and exhibited a high correlation of R2 =0.98. A plateau was observed at a glucose concentration of less than 100μM. Furthermore, in Fig. 6c, free GOx also exhibited a linear relationship between (I0-I)/I0 and glucose concentration in the range of 88–400μM (R2 =0.96). All results for glucose detection for glucose were 100μM (S/N=3). These result indicate that GPS-SANDs are a good candidate for GOx immobilization, and that the GOx activity is maintained. Moreover, this platform was able to detect glucose and further applied for biosensing with enzymatic systems.

A new epoxide-containing nanomaterial (GPS-SANDs) has been successfully prepared through a facile one-step heat treatment process for use as a biosensor. The GPS-SANDs particles were spherical-like particles with an average diameter of 3.1±0.3nm. GPS-SANDs exhibited a broad band (480­–580nm) and multicolor fluorescence. Based on aminolysis of the epoxide functional groups, GOx was immobilized on GPS-SANDs for glucose determination using PL spectrometry. After GOx was immobilized on GPS-SANDs, the PL spectra were shifted to longer wavelength. During glucose detection, an increase in the glucose concentration resulted in quenching of the PL intensity. The glucose detection limit of GOx-GPS-SANDs was determined to be 100 μM. The GPS-SANDs platform could be applied for the design of optical glucose biosensors for high sensitivity glucose detection. Therefore, this result is expected to stimulate the further development of enzyme immobilized GPS-SANDs for use as biosensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to thank the Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan (NSC 104-2628-M-110-001-MY3), the Center for Stem Cell Research, Kaohsiung Medical University, Kaohsiung, Taiwan (KMU-TP104G00, KMU-TP104G01) and the Center for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, National Sun Yat-Sen University, Kaohsiung, Taiwan for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.08.111.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Fluorescence sensing of glucose using glucose oxidase modified by PVA-pyrene prepared via click chemistry
Quantum dots and ionic liquid-sensitized effect as an efficient and green catalyst for the sensitive determination of glucose
Optical detection of glucose by means of metal nanoparticles or semiconductor quantum dots
A new approach for quantitative determination of glucose by using CdSe/ZnS quantum dots
Label-free carbon quantum dots as photoluminescence probes for ultrasensitive detection of glucose
B-doped carbon quantum dots as a sensitive fluorescence probe for hydrogen peroxide and glucose detection
Label-free Si quantum dots as photoluminescence probes for glucose detection
Water‐soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design
Enzyme biosensor systems based on porous silicon photoluminescence for detection of glucose, urea and heavy metals
A new nanobiosensor for glucose with high sensitivity and selectivity in serum based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CdTe quantum dots and Au nanoparticles
A novel enzyme-mimic nanosensor based on quantum dot-Au nanoparticle@ silica mesoporous microsphere for the detection of glucose
Graphene quantum dots as a new substrate for immobilization and direct electrochemistry of glucose oxidase: application to sensitive glucose determination
Comparison of the direct electrochemistry of glucose oxidase immobilized on the surface of Au, CdS and ZnS nanostructures
a carrier for immobilization of enzymes of industrial potential
Graphene-based nanobiocatalytic systems: recent advances and future prospects
ZnO nanorod-Based non-enzymatic optical glucose biosensor
Substrate-free self-assembled SiOx-core nanodots from alkylalkoxysilane as a multicolor photoluminescence source for intravital imaging
Aqueous self-assembly and surface-functionalized nanodots for live cell imaging and labeling
Anti-CD4-targeted gold nanoparticles induce specific contrast enhancement of peripheral lymph nodes in X-ray computed tomography of live mice
Glucose oxidase and ferrocene labels immobilized at Au/TiO 2 nanocomposites with high load amount and activity for sensitive immunoelectrochemical measurement of ProGRP biomarker
Oxidation effects on graded porous silicon anti-reflection coatings
Blood activation and compatibility on single-molecular-layer biointerfaces
Highly chemoselective addition of amines to epoxides in water
Synthesis of β-amino alcohols by ring opening of epoxides with amines catalyzed by cyanuric chloride under mild and solvent-free conditions
Optimization of gold nanoparticle photoluminescence by alkanethiolation
Highly fluorescent water-soluble, size-tunable gold quantum dots
Synthesis, characterization, and bioconjugation of fluorescent gold nanoclusters toward biological labeling applications

Covalent immobilization of β-glucosidase on magnetic particles for lignocellulose hydrolysis
Kinetic investigations of hydrolysis and condensation of the glycidoxypropyltrimethoxysilane/aminopropyltriethoxy-silane system by means of FT-Raman spectroscopy I
Raman spectroscopy of proteins: a review
Development of piezoelectric immunosensors for measurement of albuminuria
B-doped carbon quantum dots as a sensitive fluorescence probe for hydrogen peroxide and glucose detection

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Pei-Ying Lin is a Ph.D. candidate under the supervision of Professor Shuchen Hsieh in the department of chemistry at National Sun Yat-Sen University. Her research interest is focused on the synthesis and modification of luminescent and magnetic nanomaterials.

Shuchen Hsieh received her Ph.D. degree in Physical Chemistry from University of Southern California, Los Angeles, CA in 2003 under Prof. Bruce E. Koel. She joined Department of Chemistry as an assistant professor in 2006. Since 2011, she has been an Associated professor and promoted as Professor in 2015. Her research interest covers many aspects of Surface Chemistry and Scanning Probe Microscopy Atomic Force Microscopy. Her current research interest is mainly focused on the development of new Self-Assembled Monolayers, Nanomaterial Synthesis and Applications and Green Chemistry.
